title: FPGA Trading Pipeline Simulator
description: Build a complete simulated FPGA trading pipeline: market data decoding, hardware-style order book, market-making strategy with fixed-point arithmetic, and pre-trade risk checks. Analyze tick-to-trade latency at nanosecond granularity.
learningObjectives: Decode binary wire-protocol messages into structured events with nanosecond timing
Build a hardware-style order book using pre-allocated arrays with O(1) access
Implement a market-making strategy using only integer/fixed-point arithmetic
Design pre-trade risk checks and analyze tick-to-trade latency budgets
Understand why FPGAs dominate ultra-low-latency trading infrastructure

milestones.title: Market Data Feed Parser
milestones.description: Decode simulated FAST-protocol binary messages into structured order events. Every nanosecond counts on the wire: your decoder must parse raw bytes into typed events with fixed-point prices, tracking decode latency in simulated clock cycles. In real FPGA systems, this stage runs in under 30ns -- your Python simulation models the same pipeline structure.
concepts: Fixed-point arithmetic (integer representation of decimal prices)
Wire-speed message decoding with latency tracking
milestones.starterCode:
import struct
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import IntEnum
from typing import List, Optional, Tuple


class MessageType(IntEnum):
    """Wire protocol message types.
    Each maps to a single byte on the wire.
    Real FAST protocol uses tag-value pairs; we simplify to fixed-format."""
    ADD = 0x01
    MODIFY = 0x02
    CANCEL = 0x03
    EXECUTE = 0x04


class Side(IntEnum):
    """Order side. BID=0x00 (buy), ASK=0x01 (sell)."""
    BID = 0x00
    ASK = 0x01


class FixedPoint:
    """Fixed-point arithmetic with 4 decimal places.
    
    In hardware, all prices are integers. $150.0025 is stored as 1500025.
    This avoids IEEE 754 rounding errors that are unacceptable in finance.
    
    RULE: No floating-point operations. All math is integer math.
    The only float conversion is for display purposes via to_float().
    
    Examples:
        price = FixedPoint(1500025)  # Represents $150.0025
        price2 = FixedPoint(1500050) # Represents $150.0050
        spread = price2 - price     # FixedPoint(25) = $0.0025
    """
    SCALE = 10000  # 4 decimal places
    
    __slots__ = ('_value',)
    
    def __init__(self, int_value: int):
        if not isinstance(int_value, int):
            raise TypeError(f"FixedPoint requires int, got {type(int_value).__name__}")
        self._value = int_value
    
    @classmethod
    def from_dollars(cls, dollars: int, cents: int = 0, sub_cents: int = 0) -> 'FixedPoint':
        """Construct from dollar components. All arguments must be integers.
        
        Args:
            dollars: Whole dollar amount
            cents: Cents (0-99)
            sub_cents: Sub-cent amount in hundredths of a cent (0-99)
            
        Example:
            FixedPoint.from_dollars(150, 25, 50)  # $150.2550
        """
        return cls(dollars * cls.SCALE + cents * 100 + sub_cents)
    
    @property
    def value(self) -> int:
        return self._value
    
    def to_float(self) -> float:
        """Display-only conversion. NEVER use for calculations."""
        return self._value / self.SCALE
    
    def __add__(self, other: 'FixedPoint') -> 'FixedPoint':
        return FixedPoint(self._value + other._value)
    
    def __sub__(self, other: 'FixedPoint') -> 'FixedPoint':
        return FixedPoint(self._value - other._value)
    
    def __mul__(self, scalar: int) -> 'FixedPoint':
        """Multiply by integer scalar only."""
        if not isinstance(scalar, int):
            raise TypeError("FixedPoint can only multiply by int")
        return FixedPoint(self._value * scalar)
    
    def __floordiv__(self, divisor: int) -> 'FixedPoint':
        """Integer division only."""
        if not isinstance(divisor, int):
            raise TypeError("FixedPoint can only divide by int")
        return FixedPoint(self._value // divisor)
    
    def __eq__(self, other) -> bool:
        if isinstance(other, FixedPoint):
            return self._value == other._value
        return NotImplemented
    
    def __lt__(self, other: 'FixedPoint') -> bool:
        return self._value < other._value
    
    def __le__(self, other: 'FixedPoint') -> bool:
        return self._value <= other._value
    
    def __gt__(self, other: 'FixedPoint') -> bool:
        return self._value > other._value
    
    def __ge__(self, other: 'FixedPoint') -> bool:
        return self._value >= other._value
    
    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        sign = '-' if self._value < 0 else ''
        abs_val = abs(self._value)
        whole = abs_val // self.SCALE
        frac = abs_val % self.SCALE
        return f"FixedPoint({sign}{whole}.{frac:04d})"
    
    def __hash__(self) -> int:
        return hash(self._value)


@dataclass
class OrderEvent:
    """A decoded market data event.
    
    This is the output of the feed decoder: a structured representation
    of a binary wire message. In hardware, this would be a set of
    parallel signal lines out of the decoder stage.
    
    Attributes:
        msg_type: ADD, MODIFY, CANCEL, or EXECUTE
        order_id: Unique 64-bit order identifier
        side: BID or ASK
        price_fixed: Price in fixed-point (integer * 10000)
        quantity: Number of shares
        timestamp_ns: Exchange timestamp in nanoseconds since midnight
    """
    msg_type: MessageType
    order_id: int
    side: Side
    price_fixed: FixedPoint
    quantity: int
    timestamp_ns: int


# Wire format specification:
# Total message size: 26 bytes, fixed-width, big-endian
# Offset  Size  Field         Type     Notes
# 0       1     msg_type      uint8    MessageType enum value
# 1       8     order_id      uint64   Unique order identifier
# 9       1     side          uint8    0x00=BID, 0x01=ASK
# 10      4     price         int32    Fixed-point price (value / 10000)
# 14      4     quantity      uint32   Share count
# 18      8     timestamp_ns  uint64   Nanoseconds since midnight
#                                       Total: 26 bytes

MESSAGE_SIZE = 26
MESSAGE_FORMAT = '>BQBiIQ'  # big-endian: uint8, uint64, uint8, int32, uint32, uint64


class SimulatedWire:
    """Simulates a network interface card (NIC) receiving raw bytes.
    
    In a real system, an FPGA sits directly on the network card.
    Raw Ethernet frames arrive at wire speed (10Gbps = 1 byte per 0.8ns).
    This class simulates that stream of bytes with timing.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, messages: List[bytes]):
        self._buffer = b''.join(messages)
        self._offset = 0
        self._start_ns = 0  # Simulated clock
        self._bytes_per_ns = 1.25  # 10Gbps line rate
    
    def has_data(self) -> bool:
        return self._offset < len(self._buffer)
    
    def read_message(self) -> Tuple[bytes, int]:
        """Read next message from wire. Returns (raw_bytes, arrival_timestamp_ns)."""
        if not self.has_data():
            raise StopIteration("No more data on wire")
        
        raw = self._buffer[self._offset:self._offset + MESSAGE_SIZE]
        arrival_ns = int(self._offset / self._bytes_per_ns)
        self._offset += MESSAGE_SIZE
        return raw, arrival_ns
    
    def total_messages(self) -> int:
        return len(self._buffer) // MESSAGE_SIZE


def encode_message(msg_type: MessageType, order_id: int, side: Side,
                   price_fixed: int, quantity: int, timestamp_ns: int) -> bytes:
    """Encode an order event into wire-format bytes. Used to generate test data."""
    return struct.pack(MESSAGE_FORMAT, msg_type, order_id, side,
                       price_fixed, quantity, timestamp_ns)


class FeedDecoder:
    """Decodes binary wire messages into structured OrderEvent objects.
    
    In an FPGA, this is a combinational logic block that maps
    input bytes to output signal lines in a single clock cycle.
    Our simulation tracks the decode latency in simulated cycles.
    
    Performance target: < 30ns per message (simulated)
    
    Attributes:
        messages_decoded: Total messages successfully decoded
        total_decode_cycles: Sum of all decode latencies
        decode_errors: Count of malformed messages
    """
    
    SIMULATED_DECODE_NS = 30  # Target: 30ns per message in hardware
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.messages_decoded: int = 0
        self.total_decode_cycles: int = 0
        self.decode_errors: int = 0
        self._latencies: List[int] = []
    
    def decode_message(self, raw_bytes: bytes) -> Optional[OrderEvent]:
        """Decode a single binary message into an OrderEvent.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Steps:
        1. Validate message length (must be MESSAGE_SIZE bytes)
        2. Unpack binary fields using struct.unpack with MESSAGE_FORMAT
        3. Validate msg_type is a valid MessageType
        4. Validate side is a valid Side
        5. Construct FixedPoint price from the int32 price field
        6. Build and return OrderEvent
        7. Track decode latency (use SIMULATED_DECODE_NS)
        8. On any error, increment decode_errors and return None
        
        Args:
            raw_bytes: Raw binary message (26 bytes)
            
        Returns:
            OrderEvent if valid, None if malformed
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def decode_stream(self, wire: SimulatedWire) -> List[OrderEvent]:
        """Decode all messages from a simulated wire.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Steps:
        1. Read messages from wire until no more data
        2. Decode each message
        3. Track per-message decode latency
        4. Return list of successfully decoded events (skip None results)
        
        Returns:
            List of decoded OrderEvents in wire order
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def average_decode_ns(self) -> int:
        """Average decode latency in nanoseconds."""
        if not self._latencies:
            return 0
        return sum(self._latencies) // len(self._latencies)
    
    def get_latency_histogram(self) -> dict:
        """Returns histogram of decode latencies."""
        histogram = {}
        for lat in self._latencies:
            bucket = (lat // 10) * 10  # 10ns buckets
            histogram[bucket] = histogram.get(bucket, 0) + 1
        return histogram
milestones.testCode:
# --- Milestone 1 Tests: Market Data Feed Parser ---
import struct

# Test 1: Decode ADD message
raw_add = encode_message(MessageType.ADD, 1001, Side.BID, 1500025, 100, 1000000)
decoder = FeedDecoder()
event = decoder.decode_message(raw_add)
assert event is not None, "Failed to decode ADD message"
assert event.msg_type == MessageType.ADD, f"Expected ADD, got {event.msg_type}"
assert event.order_id == 1001, f"Expected order_id 1001, got {event.order_id}"
assert event.side == Side.BID, f"Expected BID, got {event.side}"
assert event.price_fixed == FixedPoint(1500025), f"Expected price 1500025, got {event.price_fixed}"
assert event.quantity == 100, f"Expected quantity 100, got {event.quantity}"
print("Test 1 passed: ADD message decoded correctly")

# Test 2: Decode MODIFY message
raw_mod = encode_message(MessageType.MODIFY, 1001, Side.BID, 1500025, 200, 2000000)
event2 = decoder.decode_message(raw_mod)
assert event2 is not None, "Failed to decode MODIFY message"
assert event2.msg_type == MessageType.MODIFY, f"Expected MODIFY, got {event2.msg_type}"
assert event2.quantity == 200, f"Expected quantity 200, got {event2.quantity}"
print("Test 2 passed: MODIFY message decoded correctly")

# Test 3: Decode CANCEL message
raw_cancel = encode_message(MessageType.CANCEL, 1002, Side.ASK, 1500100, 0, 3000000)
event3 = decoder.decode_message(raw_cancel)
assert event3 is not None, "Failed to decode CANCEL message"
assert event3.msg_type == MessageType.CANCEL, f"Expected CANCEL, got {event3.msg_type}"
print("Test 3 passed: CANCEL message decoded correctly")

# Test 4: Decode EXECUTE message
raw_exec = encode_message(MessageType.EXECUTE, 1003, Side.BID, 1500050, 50, 4000000)
event4 = decoder.decode_message(raw_exec)
assert event4 is not None, "Failed to decode EXECUTE message"
assert event4.msg_type == MessageType.EXECUTE, f"Expected EXECUTE, got {event4.msg_type}"
assert event4.quantity == 50, f"Expected quantity 50, got {event4.quantity}"
print("Test 4 passed: EXECUTE message decoded correctly")

# Test 5: Fixed-point price conversion
price = FixedPoint(1500025)
assert price.value == 1500025, f"Expected raw value 1500025, got {price.value}"
assert abs(price.to_float() - 150.0025) < 1e-9, f"Expected 150.0025, got {price.to_float()}"
price2 = FixedPoint.from_dollars(150, 0, 25)
assert price == price2, "from_dollars(150, 0, 25) should equal FixedPoint(1500025)"
print("Test 5 passed: Fixed-point price conversion correct")

# Test 6: Fixed-point arithmetic uses only integers
spread = FixedPoint(1500100) - FixedPoint(1500000)
assert spread.value == 100, f"Expected spread of 100 (=$0.01), got {spread.value}"
scaled = FixedPoint(1000) * 3
assert scaled.value == 3000, f"Expected 3000, got {scaled.value}"
half = FixedPoint(1000) // 2
assert half.value == 500, f"Expected 500, got {half.value}"
try:
    _ = FixedPoint(1000) * 1.5
    assert False, "Should have raised TypeError for float multiply"
except TypeError:
    pass
print("Test 6 passed: Fixed-point arithmetic is integer-only")

# Test 7: Reject malformed messages
short_msg = b'\x01\x02\x03'  # Too short
result = decoder.decode_message(short_msg)
assert result is None, "Should reject short message"
assert decoder.decode_errors >= 1, "Should increment error count"
bad_type = encode_message(0xFF, 1, 0, 1500000, 100, 0)
result2 = decoder.decode_message(bad_type)
assert result2 is None, "Should reject invalid message type"
print("Test 7 passed: Malformed messages rejected")

# Test 8: Stream decoding and latency tracking
messages = [
    encode_message(MessageType.ADD, i, Side.BID if i % 2 == 0 else Side.ASK,
                   1500000 + i * 25, 100 + i, i * 1000000)
    for i in range(50)
]
wire = SimulatedWire(messages)
stream_decoder = FeedDecoder()
events = stream_decoder.decode_stream(wire)
assert len(events) == 50, f"Expected 50 events, got {len(events)}"
assert stream_decoder.messages_decoded == 50, f"Expected 50 decoded, got {stream_decoder.messages_decoded}"
assert stream_decoder.average_decode_ns() == 30, f"Expected 30ns avg, got {stream_decoder.average_decode_ns()}"
assert events[0].order_id == 0, f"Expected first order_id 0, got {events[0].order_id}"
assert events[49].order_id == 49, f"Expected last order_id 49, got {events[49].order_id}"
print("Test 8 passed: Stream decoding with latency tracking")

print("\n=== ALL MILESTONE 1 TESTS PASSED ===")
hints: Use struct.unpack(MESSAGE_FORMAT, raw_bytes) to extract all fields in one call. The format string '>BQBiIQ' maps directly to the wire layout.
Wrap msg_type and side parsing in try/except ValueError blocks -- MessageType(0xFF) will raise ValueError for invalid enum values.
For decode_stream, use a simple while loop: while wire.has_data(), read raw bytes, decode, append non-None results. The wire handles its own offset.

milestones.title: Hardware-Style Order Book
milestones.description: Build an order book that mirrors how FPGAs actually maintain market state. Unlike software order books that use tree structures (std::map in C++), FPGA order books use pre-allocated arrays indexed by price level. You get O(1) access at the cost of fixed memory: a 256-level array per side covers a $2.56 price range at $0.01 granularity. Process Add/Modify/Cancel/Execute events and maintain best bid/ask, spread, depth, and VWAP -- all in fixed-point.
concepts: Array-based order book (hardware constraint: no dynamic allocation)
Direct price-to-index mapping for O(1) access
Best bid/ask tracking and spread computation
milestones.starterCode:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional, Dict
# Assumes Milestone 1 classes are available:
# MessageType, Side, FixedPoint, OrderEvent


NUM_PRICE_LEVELS = 256  # Fixed array size per side (hardware constraint)


@dataclass
class Order:
    """A single order resting in the book."""
    order_id: int
    side: Side
    price_fixed: FixedPoint
    quantity: int
    timestamp_ns: int


@dataclass
class PriceLevel:
    """One price level in the order book.
    
    In hardware, this is a register bank: price, total quantity,
    order count, and a small FIFO of individual orders.
    """
    price: FixedPoint
    total_quantity: int = 0
    order_count: int = 0
    orders: List[Order] = field(default_factory=list)
    
    def is_empty(self) -> bool:
        return self.total_quantity == 0


@dataclass
class BookSnapshot:
    """A point-in-time snapshot of the order book top-of-book.
    
    Attributes:
        best_bid: Highest bid price (None if no bids)
        best_ask: Lowest ask price (None if no asks)
        spread: Ask - Bid in fixed-point (None if one side empty)
        bid_depth: Total quantity across all bid levels
        ask_depth: Total quantity across all ask levels
        bid_levels: Number of non-empty bid price levels
        ask_levels: Number of non-empty ask price levels
        bid_vwap: Volume-weighted average bid price (fixed-point)
        ask_vwap: Volume-weighted average ask price (fixed-point)
    """
    best_bid: Optional[FixedPoint]
    best_ask: Optional[FixedPoint]
    spread: Optional[FixedPoint]
    bid_depth: int
    ask_depth: int
    bid_levels: int
    ask_levels: int
    bid_vwap: Optional[FixedPoint]
    ask_vwap: Optional[FixedPoint]


class OrderBook:
    """Hardware-style order book using pre-allocated arrays.
    
    Key design constraint: FPGAs cannot dynamically allocate memory.
    Everything is pre-allocated at synthesis time. We use fixed-size
    arrays indexed by price level, giving O(1) access to any price.
    
    Price-to-index mapping:
        index = (price.value - base_price.value) // tick_size.value
        
    With tick_size = $0.01 (100 in fixed-point) and 256 levels,
    we cover a $2.56 range per side centered around a reference price.
    
    Attributes:
        base_price: Lowest price in the bid array
        tick_size: Minimum price increment (in fixed-point units)
        events_processed: Total events processed
    """
    
    def __init__(self, reference_price: FixedPoint,
                 tick_size: FixedPoint = FixedPoint(100)):
        """Initialize pre-allocated order book arrays.
        
        Args:
            reference_price: Center price for the book
            tick_size: Minimum price increment ($0.01 default)
        """
        self.tick_size = tick_size
        self.reference_price = reference_price
        
        # Bid array: prices from (ref - 128*tick) to (ref - 1*tick)
        # Ask array: prices from (ref) to (ref + 127*tick)
        self.bid_base = FixedPoint(reference_price.value - NUM_PRICE_LEVELS * tick_size.value)
        self.ask_base = reference_price
        
        # Pre-allocate arrays (this is what FPGA block RAM looks like)
        self.bid_levels: List[PriceLevel] = [
            PriceLevel(price=FixedPoint(self.bid_base.value + i * tick_size.value))
            for i in range(NUM_PRICE_LEVELS)
        ]
        self.ask_levels: List[PriceLevel] = [
            PriceLevel(price=FixedPoint(self.ask_base.value + i * tick_size.value))
            for i in range(NUM_PRICE_LEVELS)
        ]
        
        # Order lookup: order_id -> (side, price_level_index)
        # In hardware, this would be a CAM (content-addressable memory)
        self._order_map: Dict[int, Tuple[Side, int]] = {}
        
        self.events_processed: int = 0
    
    def price_to_index(self, price: FixedPoint, side: Side) -> Optional[int]:
        """Map a fixed-point price to an array index.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        For BID side: index = (price.value - bid_base.value) // tick_size.value
        For ASK side: index = (price.value - ask_base.value) // tick_size.value
        
        Return None if index is out of range [0, NUM_PRICE_LEVELS).
        
        Args:
            price: Fixed-point price
            side: BID or ASK
            
        Returns:
            Array index, or None if price is outside the book range
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def process_event(self, event: OrderEvent) -> bool:
        """Process a single order event and update book state.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Dispatch to the appropriate handler based on event.msg_type:
          ADD -> _handle_add
          MODIFY -> _handle_modify
          CANCEL -> _handle_cancel
          EXECUTE -> _handle_execute
        
        Increment events_processed on success.
        
        Returns:
            True if event was processed successfully, False otherwise
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def _handle_add(self, event: OrderEvent) -> bool:
        """Add a new order to the book.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Steps:
        1. Map price to array index using price_to_index
        2. Create Order object
        3. Append to price level's orders list
        4. Update total_quantity and order_count
        5. Store in _order_map for fast lookup on modify/cancel
        
        Returns:
            True on success, False if price is out of range
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def _handle_modify(self, event: OrderEvent) -> bool:
        """Modify an existing order's quantity.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Steps:
        1. Look up order in _order_map
        2. Find the order in the price level
        3. Update the price level's total_quantity (subtract old, add new)
        4. Update the order's quantity
        
        Returns:
            True on success, False if order not found
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def _handle_cancel(self, event: OrderEvent) -> bool:
        """Cancel (remove) an order from the book.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Steps:
        1. Look up order in _order_map
        2. Remove from price level's orders list
        3. Update total_quantity and order_count
        4. Remove from _order_map
        
        Returns:
            True on success, False if order not found
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def _handle_execute(self, event: OrderEvent) -> bool:
        """Execute (fill) an order partially or fully.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Steps:
        1. Look up order in _order_map
        2. Reduce order quantity by event quantity
        3. Reduce price level total_quantity
        4. If order quantity reaches 0, remove it entirely
        
        Returns:
            True on success, False if order not found
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def get_best_bid(self) -> Optional[FixedPoint]:
        """Find highest non-empty bid price level.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        Scan bid_levels from highest index down. Return price of first
        non-empty level. Return None if all levels empty.
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def get_best_ask(self) -> Optional[FixedPoint]:
        """Find lowest non-empty ask price level.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        Scan ask_levels from lowest index up. Return price of first
        non-empty level. Return None if all levels empty.
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def compute_vwap(self, side: Side, depth: int = 0) -> Optional[FixedPoint]:
        """Compute volume-weighted average price for a side.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        VWAP = sum(price_i * quantity_i) / sum(quantity_i)
        
        ALL ARITHMETIC MUST BE INTEGER (fixed-point).
        
        For VWAP in fixed-point:
          numerator = sum(level.price.value * level.total_quantity)
          denominator = sum(level.total_quantity)
          vwap = FixedPoint(numerator // denominator)
        
        Args:
            side: BID or ASK
            depth: If > 0, only consider top N levels. 0 = all levels.
            
        Returns:
            VWAP as FixedPoint, or None if side is empty
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def snapshot(self) -> BookSnapshot:
        """Capture current top-of-book state.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        Build a BookSnapshot with best_bid, best_ask, spread,
        bid/ask depth, level counts, and VWAPs.
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
milestones.testCode:
# --- Milestone 2 Tests: Hardware-Style Order Book ---

ref_price = FixedPoint(1500000)  # $150.00
book = OrderBook(reference_price=ref_price)

# Test 1: Add bid orders at different prices
add1 = OrderEvent(MessageType.ADD, 2001, Side.BID, FixedPoint(1499900), 100, 1000000)  # $149.99
add2 = OrderEvent(MessageType.ADD, 2002, Side.BID, FixedPoint(1499800), 200, 1000100)  # $149.98
assert book.process_event(add1), "Failed to add bid order 2001"
assert book.process_event(add2), "Failed to add bid order 2002"
assert book.get_best_bid() == FixedPoint(1499900), f"Expected best bid $149.99, got {book.get_best_bid()}"
print("Test 1 passed: Bid orders added, best bid correct")

# Test 2: Add ask orders
add3 = OrderEvent(MessageType.ADD, 2003, Side.ASK, FixedPoint(1500100), 150, 1000200)  # $150.01
add4 = OrderEvent(MessageType.ADD, 2004, Side.ASK, FixedPoint(1500200), 250, 1000300)  # $150.02
assert book.process_event(add3), "Failed to add ask order 2003"
assert book.process_event(add4), "Failed to add ask order 2004"
assert book.get_best_ask() == FixedPoint(1500100), f"Expected best ask $150.01, got {book.get_best_ask()}"
print("Test 2 passed: Ask orders added, best ask correct")

# Test 3: Verify spread
snap = book.snapshot()
assert snap.spread == FixedPoint(200), f"Expected spread $0.02 (200), got {snap.spread}"
print("Test 3 passed: Spread calculation correct")

# Test 4: Modify order quantity
mod1 = OrderEvent(MessageType.MODIFY, 2001, Side.BID, FixedPoint(1499900), 300, 2000000)
assert book.process_event(mod1), "Failed to modify order 2001"
snap2 = book.snapshot()
assert snap2.bid_depth == 500, f"Expected bid depth 500 (300+200), got {snap2.bid_depth}"
print("Test 4 passed: Order modification correct")

# Test 5: Cancel order
cancel1 = OrderEvent(MessageType.CANCEL, 2002, Side.BID, FixedPoint(1499800), 0, 3000000)
assert book.process_event(cancel1), "Failed to cancel order 2002"
snap3 = book.snapshot()
assert snap3.bid_depth == 300, f"Expected bid depth 300 after cancel, got {snap3.bid_depth}"
assert snap3.bid_levels == 1, f"Expected 1 bid level after cancel, got {snap3.bid_levels}"
print("Test 5 passed: Order cancellation correct")

# Test 6: Execute (partial fill)
exec1 = OrderEvent(MessageType.EXECUTE, 2003, Side.ASK, FixedPoint(1500100), 50, 4000000)
assert book.process_event(exec1), "Failed to execute order 2003"
snap4 = book.snapshot()
assert snap4.ask_depth == 350, f"Expected ask depth 350 (100+250), got {snap4.ask_depth}"
assert snap4.best_ask == FixedPoint(1500100), "Best ask should remain after partial fill"
print("Test 6 passed: Partial execution correct")

# Test 7: Execute (full fill removes order)
exec2 = OrderEvent(MessageType.EXECUTE, 2003, Side.ASK, FixedPoint(1500100), 100, 5000000)
assert book.process_event(exec2), "Failed to fully execute order 2003"
snap5 = book.snapshot()
assert snap5.best_ask == FixedPoint(1500200), f"Best ask should move to $150.02 after full fill, got {snap5.best_ask}"
assert snap5.ask_depth == 250, f"Expected ask depth 250, got {snap5.ask_depth}"
print("Test 7 passed: Full execution removes order, best ask updates")

# Test 8: VWAP calculation (integer arithmetic)
book2 = OrderBook(reference_price=FixedPoint(1500000))
book2.process_event(OrderEvent(MessageType.ADD, 3001, Side.BID, FixedPoint(1499900), 100, 0))
book2.process_event(OrderEvent(MessageType.ADD, 3002, Side.BID, FixedPoint(1499800), 200, 0))
bid_vwap = book2.compute_vwap(Side.BID)
# VWAP = (1499900*100 + 1499800*200) / 300 = (149990000 + 299960000) / 300 = 1499833
expected_vwap = (1499900 * 100 + 1499800 * 200) // 300
assert bid_vwap == FixedPoint(expected_vwap), f"Expected VWAP {expected_vwap}, got {bid_vwap}"
print("Test 8 passed: VWAP calculation with integer arithmetic")

# Test 9: Multiple orders at same price level
book3 = OrderBook(reference_price=FixedPoint(1500000))
book3.process_event(OrderEvent(MessageType.ADD, 4001, Side.ASK, FixedPoint(1500100), 100, 0))
book3.process_event(OrderEvent(MessageType.ADD, 4002, Side.ASK, FixedPoint(1500100), 200, 0))
snap6 = book3.snapshot()
assert snap6.ask_depth == 300, f"Expected ask depth 300, got {snap6.ask_depth}"
assert snap6.ask_levels == 1, f"Expected 1 ask level, got {snap6.ask_levels}"
print("Test 9 passed: Multiple orders at same price level")

# Test 10: Out-of-range price rejected
far_order = OrderEvent(MessageType.ADD, 5001, Side.BID, FixedPoint(1000000), 100, 0)  # $100 -- way out of range
assert not book3.process_event(far_order), "Should reject out-of-range price"
print("Test 10 passed: Out-of-range price rejected")

# Test 11: Events processed counter
assert book.events_processed >= 6, f"Expected at least 6 events processed, got {book.events_processed}"
print("Test 11 passed: Events processed counter")

print("\n=== ALL MILESTONE 2 TESTS PASSED ===")
hints: For price_to_index: compute (price.value - base.value) // tick_size.value. The base is bid_base for BID side, ask_base for ASK side. Check 0 <= index < NUM_PRICE_LEVELS.
For _handle_execute: use min(event.quantity, order.quantity) as the fill quantity. If the order is fully filled (quantity reaches 0), remove it from the level and _order_map.
For VWAP: accumulate numerator = sum(price.value * qty) and denominator = sum(qty) across non-empty levels. Return FixedPoint(numerator // denominator). ALL integer math.

milestones.title: Market-Making Strategy
milestones.description: Implement a market-making strategy that quotes bid and ask prices around the mid-price. The critical constraint: NO floating-point arithmetic. Real FPGA strategies use only integer/fixed-point math because floating-point units consume too many logic elements and add latency. Your strategy must compute mid-price, apply a base spread, skew quotes based on inventory, track position and P&L -- all with integer operations. This is where you learn why quant firms hire hardware engineers.
concepts: Market-making: quoting both sides of the book
Inventory skew: adjusting quotes to manage risk
milestones.starterCode:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional, Tuple
# Assumes Milestone 1-2 classes are available


@dataclass
class StrategyConfig:
    """Market-making strategy parameters.
    
    All values are in basis points (bps) or integer units.
    1 basis point = 0.01% = 0.0001 of price.
    In fixed-point with SCALE=10000: 1 bps of $150.00 = 150000 * 1 // 10000 = 15
    
    Attributes:
        base_spread_bps: Base half-spread in basis points (e.g., 10 bps = 0.1%)
        max_position: Maximum allowed net position (shares)
        skew_factor_bps: Basis points of spread adjustment per unit of inventory
        min_quote_size: Minimum quantity to quote
        max_quote_size: Maximum quantity to quote
        position_limit: Hard position limit (cancel all quotes if breached)
    """
    base_spread_bps: int = 10       # 10 bps = $0.15 on a $150 stock
    max_position: int = 1000
    skew_factor_bps: int = 1        # 1 bps skew per share of inventory
    min_quote_size: int = 50
    max_quote_size: int = 500
    position_limit: int = 2000


@dataclass
class QuoteOrder:
    """An order to be sent to the exchange."""
    side: Side
    price_fixed: FixedPoint
    quantity: int


@dataclass
class QuotePair:
    """A pair of bid/ask quotes generated by the strategy."""
    bid: Optional[QuoteOrder]
    ask: Optional[QuoteOrder]
    mid_price: FixedPoint
    bid_spread_bps: int
    ask_spread_bps: int
    reason: str  # Human-readable reason for this quote decision


@dataclass
class Fill:
    """Record of a trade execution."""
    side: Side  # Side WE were on
    price_fixed: FixedPoint
    quantity: int
    timestamp_ns: int


class PositionTracker:
    """Tracks net position and P&L using fixed-point arithmetic.
    
    Position convention:
      - Positive = long (we own shares)
      - Negative = short (we owe shares)
    
    P&L calculation:
      When we BUY:  position += qty, cash -= price * qty
      When we SELL: position -= qty, cash += price * qty
      
      Realized P&L = cash (in fixed-point * quantity units)
      Unrealized P&L = position * current_price (fixed-point)
      Total P&L = realized + unrealized
    
    ALL arithmetic is integer. Cash is tracked in fixed-point-quantity
    units (price.value * quantity), so to get dollar P&L you divide
    by FixedPoint.SCALE at display time only.
    """
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.net_position: int = 0
        self.cash_fp: int = 0  # Fixed-point * quantity units
        self.total_bought: int = 0
        self.total_sold: int = 0
        self.trade_count: int = 0
        self.fills: List[Fill] = []
    
    def record_fill(self, fill: Fill) -> None:
        """Record a trade fill and update position/cash.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        If fill.side == BID (we bought):
            net_position += fill.quantity
            cash_fp -= fill.price_fixed.value * fill.quantity
            total_bought += fill.quantity
        If fill.side == ASK (we sold):
            net_position -= fill.quantity
            cash_fp += fill.price_fixed.value * fill.quantity
            total_sold += fill.quantity
        
        Always increment trade_count and append to fills.
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def realized_pnl_fp(self) -> int:
        """Realized P&L in fixed-point units. Divide by SCALE for dollars."""
        return self.cash_fp
    
    def unrealized_pnl_fp(self, current_price: FixedPoint) -> int:
        """Unrealized P&L given current mid-price."""
        return self.net_position * current_price.value
    
    def total_pnl_fp(self, current_price: FixedPoint) -> int:
        """Total P&L = realized + unrealized (fixed-point units)."""
        return self.realized_pnl_fp() + self.unrealized_pnl_fp(current_price)
    
    def pnl_dollars(self, current_price: FixedPoint) -> float:
        """Display-only: total P&L in dollars."""
        return self.total_pnl_fp(current_price) / FixedPoint.SCALE


class TradingStrategy:
    """Fixed-point market-making strategy.
    
    Generates bid/ask quotes around the mid-price.
    Adjusts spread based on inventory position.
    
    Key constraint: ALL arithmetic must be integer.
    No float, no Decimal -- just int and FixedPoint.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, config: StrategyConfig):
        self.config = config
        self.position = PositionTracker()
        self.quotes_generated: int = 0
        self.quotes_suppressed: int = 0
    
    def compute_mid_price(self, best_bid: FixedPoint,
                          best_ask: FixedPoint) -> FixedPoint:
        """Compute mid-price using integer arithmetic.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        mid = (best_bid.value + best_ask.value) // 2
        
        Returns:
            Mid-price as FixedPoint
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def compute_spread_bps(self) -> Tuple[int, int]:
        """Compute bid/ask spread in basis points, adjusted for inventory.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Base spread: config.base_spread_bps on each side.
        
        Inventory skew:
          - If net_position > 0 (long), we want to SELL more:
              bid_spread += abs(position) * skew_factor_bps  (widen bid = buy less aggressively)
              ask_spread -= abs(position) * skew_factor_bps  (tighten ask = sell more aggressively)
          - If net_position < 0 (short), we want to BUY more:
              bid_spread -= abs(position) * skew_factor_bps  (tighten bid)
              ask_spread += abs(position) * skew_factor_bps  (widen ask)
        
        Minimum spread on either side: 1 bps
        
        Returns:
            Tuple of (bid_spread_bps, ask_spread_bps)
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def bps_to_price_offset(self, mid_price: FixedPoint, bps: int) -> int:
        """Convert basis points to a fixed-point price offset.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        offset = mid_price.value * bps // 10000
        
        This is integer arithmetic: bps/10000 of the price.
        
        Returns:
            Price offset as integer (in fixed-point units)
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def compute_quote_size(self) -> int:
        """Compute quote size based on position.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Base size: config.max_quote_size
        Reduce size as position approaches limit:
          remaining_capacity = config.max_position - abs(net_position)
          quote_size = min(config.max_quote_size, max(config.min_quote_size, remaining_capacity))
        
        Returns:
            Number of shares to quote
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def on_book_update(self, snapshot: BookSnapshot) -> Optional[QuotePair]:
        """Generate quotes based on current book state.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Steps:
        1. If best_bid or best_ask is None, suppress (return None)
        2. If abs(net_position) >= position_limit, suppress
        3. Compute mid_price
        4. Compute bid/ask spread with inventory skew
        5. Convert spreads to price offsets
        6. Compute quote prices:
             bid_price = mid_price.value - bid_offset
             ask_price = mid_price.value + ask_offset
        7. Compute quote size
        8. Return QuotePair
        
        Returns:
            QuotePair with bid and ask quotes, or None if suppressed
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def on_fill(self, fill: Fill) -> None:
        """Process a fill notification."""
        self.position.record_fill(fill)
milestones.testCode:
# --- Milestone 3 Tests: Market-Making Strategy ---

config = StrategyConfig(
    base_spread_bps=10,
    max_position=1000,
    skew_factor_bps=1,
    min_quote_size=50,
    max_quote_size=500,
    position_limit=2000
)
strategy = TradingStrategy(config)

# Test 1: Mid-price calculation (integer only)
mid = strategy.compute_mid_price(FixedPoint(1499900), FixedPoint(1500100))
assert mid == FixedPoint(1500000), f"Expected mid $150.00, got {mid}"
assert isinstance(mid.value, int), "Mid-price must be integer"
print("Test 1 passed: Mid-price calculation (integer)")

# Test 2: Symmetric quotes with zero position
snap = BookSnapshot(
    best_bid=FixedPoint(1499900), best_ask=FixedPoint(1500100),
    spread=FixedPoint(200), bid_depth=1000, ask_depth=1000,
    bid_levels=5, ask_levels=5,
    bid_vwap=FixedPoint(1499850), ask_vwap=FixedPoint(1500150)
)
quotes = strategy.on_book_update(snap)
assert quotes is not None, "Should generate quotes"
assert quotes.bid is not None and quotes.ask is not None, "Both sides should be quoted"
bid_distance = mid.value - quotes.bid.price_fixed.value
ask_distance = quotes.ask.price_fixed.value - mid.value
assert bid_distance == ask_distance, f"Quotes should be symmetric: bid_dist={bid_distance}, ask_dist={ask_distance}"
print("Test 2 passed: Symmetric quotes with zero position")

# Test 3: Verify spread in basis points
expected_offset = 1500000 * 10 // 10000  # 10 bps of $150
assert bid_distance == expected_offset, f"Expected offset {expected_offset}, got {bid_distance}"
print("Test 3 passed: Spread matches basis point specification")

# Test 4: Inventory skew with long position
strategy2 = TradingStrategy(config)
for i in range(5):
    strategy2.on_fill(Fill(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1500000), 100, i * 1000))
assert strategy2.position.net_position == 500, f"Expected position 500, got {strategy2.position.net_position}"
bid_bps, ask_bps = strategy2.compute_spread_bps()
assert bid_bps > ask_bps, f"Long position should widen bid: bid_bps={bid_bps}, ask_bps={ask_bps}"
assert bid_bps == 10 + 500 * 1, f"Expected bid_bps=510, got {bid_bps}"
print("Test 4 passed: Inventory skew widens correct side")

# Test 5: Inventory skew with short position
strategy3 = TradingStrategy(config)
for i in range(3):
    strategy3.on_fill(Fill(Side.ASK, FixedPoint(1500000), 100, i * 1000))
assert strategy3.position.net_position == -300, f"Expected position -300, got {strategy3.position.net_position}"
bid_bps3, ask_bps3 = strategy3.compute_spread_bps()
assert ask_bps3 > bid_bps3, f"Short position should widen ask: bid={bid_bps3}, ask={ask_bps3}"
print("Test 5 passed: Short position skew correct")

# Test 6: P&L tracking (fixed-point)
tracker = PositionTracker()
tracker.record_fill(Fill(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1500000), 100, 0))  # Buy 100 @ $150.00
tracker.record_fill(Fill(Side.ASK, FixedPoint(1500500), 100, 1000))  # Sell 100 @ $150.05
assert tracker.net_position == 0, f"Expected flat, got {tracker.net_position}"
# P&L: sold at 1500500, bought at 1500000 => profit = 500 per share * 100 shares = 50000 fp-qty units
assert tracker.cash_fp == 100 * (1500500 - 1500000), f"Expected cash {100 * 500}, got {tracker.cash_fp}"
assert tracker.trade_count == 2, f"Expected 2 trades, got {tracker.trade_count}"
pnl_dollars = tracker.pnl_dollars(FixedPoint(1500000))
assert abs(pnl_dollars - 5.0) < 0.01, f"Expected $5.00 P&L, got {pnl_dollars}"
print("Test 6 passed: P&L tracking correct")

# Test 7: No floating-point in strategy calculations
# Verify all price values in quotes are integers
strategy4 = TradingStrategy(config)
quotes4 = strategy4.on_book_update(snap)
assert isinstance(quotes4.bid.price_fixed.value, int), "Bid price must be int"
assert isinstance(quotes4.ask.price_fixed.value, int), "Ask price must be int"
assert isinstance(quotes4.bid.quantity, int), "Bid quantity must be int"
assert isinstance(quotes4.mid_price.value, int), "Mid price must be int"
print("Test 7 passed: All strategy outputs are integer types")

# Test 8: Position limit suppresses quotes
strategy5 = TradingStrategy(config)
for i in range(20):
    strategy5.on_fill(Fill(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1500000), 100, i * 1000))
assert strategy5.position.net_position == 2000
result = strategy5.on_book_update(snap)
assert result is None, "Should suppress quotes when at position limit"
assert strategy5.quotes_suppressed >= 1, "Should increment suppressed counter"
print("Test 8 passed: Position limit suppresses quotes")

# Test 9: Quote size reduces near position limit
strategy6 = TradingStrategy(config)
for i in range(9):
    strategy6.on_fill(Fill(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1500000), 100, i * 1000))
assert strategy6.position.net_position == 900
qty = strategy6.compute_quote_size()
assert qty == 100, f"Expected quote size 100 (1000-900), got {qty}"
print("Test 9 passed: Quote size adjusts near position limit")

print("\n=== ALL MILESTONE 3 TESTS PASSED ===")
hints: Mid-price: FixedPoint((bid.value + ask.value) // 2). The // 2 is integer division -- in hardware this is a 1-bit right shift, taking zero clock cycles.
For spread skew: if long (position > 0), add skew to bid_spread (buy less aggressively) and subtract from ask_spread (sell more aggressively). Clamp both to minimum 1 bps.
P&L tracking: when you BUY, cash goes DOWN (cash -= price * qty). When you SELL, cash goes UP. Net position is + for long, - for short.

milestones.title: Risk Checker and Latency Analysis
milestones.description: Build the final pipeline stages: pre-trade risk checks, outbound order encoding, and end-to-end latency analysis. In a real FPGA system, the risk checker runs in parallel with order encoding and takes ~15ns. Your pipeline simulator chains all four milestones into a single tick-to-trade path and assigns nanosecond budgets to each stage. The target: under 215ns from NIC receive to NIC transmit. This is the stage that keeps your firm out of regulatory trouble and your pipeline under budget.
concepts: Pre-trade risk management (position, rate, price, notional limits)
milestones.starterCode:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional, Dict, Tuple
from enum import Enum
# Assumes all Milestone 1-3 classes are available


class RiskRejectReason(Enum):
    """Why a risk check rejected an order."""
    POSITION_LIMIT = "position_limit_breached"
    RATE_LIMIT = "order_rate_exceeded"
    PRICE_DEVIATION = "price_too_far_from_mid"
    NOTIONAL_LIMIT = "notional_value_exceeded"
    INVALID_QUANTITY = "invalid_quantity"


@dataclass
class RiskLimits:
    """Pre-trade risk check parameters.
    
    Attributes:
        max_position: Maximum absolute net position
        max_orders_per_second: Rate limit (orders/sec)
        max_price_deviation_bps: Max deviation from mid-price in bps
        max_notional: Maximum single-order notional (price * qty in fixed-point)
        min_quantity: Minimum order quantity
        max_quantity: Maximum order quantity
    """
    max_position: int = 5000
    max_orders_per_second: int = 1000
    max_price_deviation_bps: int = 100     # 1% max deviation
    max_notional: int = 10000000000        # $1M in fixed-point (10000 * 1000000)
    min_quantity: int = 1
    max_quantity: int = 10000


@dataclass
class RiskCheckResult:
    """Result of a pre-trade risk check."""
    approved: bool
    reject_reason: Optional[RiskRejectReason] = None
    details: str = ""


class RiskChecker:
    """Pre-trade risk checks.
    
    In hardware, these run as parallel comparator circuits.
    Each check is a single clock cycle. All checks run simultaneously.
    An order is approved only if ALL checks pass.
    
    Attributes:
        orders_approved: Count of approved orders
        orders_rejected: Count of rejected orders
        rejections_by_reason: Histogram of rejection reasons
    """
    
    SIMULATED_CHECK_NS = 15  # All checks in parallel = 15ns
    
    def __init__(self, limits: RiskLimits):
        self.limits = limits
        self.orders_approved: int = 0
        self.orders_rejected: int = 0
        self.rejections_by_reason: Dict[RiskRejectReason, int] = {}
        self._order_timestamps_ns: List[int] = []  # For rate limiting
    
    def check_order(self, order: QuoteOrder, current_position: int,
                    mid_price: FixedPoint, timestamp_ns: int) -> RiskCheckResult:
        """Run all pre-trade risk checks on an order.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Checks (run in parallel in hardware, sequential here):
        
        1. Position limit: abs(current_position + signed_qty) <= max_position
           signed_qty = +qty for BID, -qty for ASK
        
        2. Rate limit: count orders in last 1 second (1_000_000_000 ns)
           if count >= max_orders_per_second, reject
        
        3. Price deviation: abs(order_price - mid_price) in bps
           deviation_bps = abs(order_price.value - mid_price.value) * 10000 // mid_price.value
           if deviation_bps > max_price_deviation_bps, reject
        
        4. Notional limit: order_price.value * quantity
           if notional > max_notional, reject
        
        5. Quantity validation: min_quantity <= quantity <= max_quantity
        
        Returns first failing check. If all pass, order is approved.
        Track statistics in orders_approved/rejected/rejections_by_reason.
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def _record_rejection(self, reason: RiskRejectReason, details: str) -> RiskCheckResult:
        """Helper to record a rejection."""
        self.orders_rejected += 1
        self.rejections_by_reason[reason] = self.rejections_by_reason.get(reason, 0) + 1
        return RiskCheckResult(approved=False, reject_reason=reason, details=details)


# Outbound wire format (same structure as inbound):
# 1-byte type | 8-byte order_id | 1-byte side | 4-byte price | 4-byte qty | 8-byte timestamp
OUTBOUND_FORMAT = '>BQBiIQ'
OUTBOUND_SIZE = 26


class OrderEncoder:
    """Encodes outbound orders into wire-format bytes.
    
    Simulated latency: 10ns per message.
    """
    
    SIMULATED_ENCODE_NS = 10
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.messages_encoded: int = 0
    
    def encode_order(self, order: QuoteOrder, order_id: int,
                     timestamp_ns: int) -> bytes:
        """Encode a QuoteOrder into wire-format bytes.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Maps QuoteOrder to the wire format:
          msg_type = MessageType.ADD (0x01) for new orders
          order_id = provided
          side = order.side
          price = order.price_fixed.value
          quantity = order.quantity
          timestamp = timestamp_ns
        
        Returns:
            26-byte encoded message
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE


@dataclass
class PipelineStage:
    """Timing record for one pipeline stage."""
    name: str
    budget_ns: int
    actual_ns: int = 0
    within_budget: bool = True


@dataclass
class LatencyReport:
    """Complete tick-to-trade latency report."""
    stages: List[PipelineStage]
    total_budget_ns: int
    total_actual_ns: int
    within_budget: bool
    
    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        lines = ["\n=== Tick-to-Trade Latency Report ==="]
        lines.append(f"{'Stage':<20} {'Budget':>8} {'Actual':>8} {'Status':>8}")
        lines.append("-" * 48)
        for s in self.stages:
            status = "OK" if s.within_budget else "OVER"
            lines.append(f"{s.name:<20} {s.budget_ns:>6}ns {s.actual_ns:>6}ns {status:>8}")
        lines.append("-" * 48)
        status = "PASS" if self.within_budget else "FAIL"
        lines.append(f"{'TOTAL':<20} {self.total_budget_ns:>6}ns {self.total_actual_ns:>6}ns {status:>8}")
        return "\n".join(lines)


class LatencyAnalyzer:
    """Tracks per-stage latency for the full pipeline.
    
    Pipeline stages and budgets:
      NIC Receive:     50ns (network interface to FPGA input)
      Feed Decode:     30ns (binary parsing)
      Book Update:     20ns (order book modification)
      Strategy:        40ns (quote calculation)
      Risk Check:      15ns (parallel comparators)
      Order Encode:    10ns (binary serialization)
      NIC Transmit:    50ns (FPGA output to network)
      Total:          215ns budget
    """
    
    STAGE_BUDGETS = {
        "nic_receive":   50,
        "feed_decode":   30,
        "book_update":   20,
        "strategy":      40,
        "risk_check":    15,
        "order_encode":  10,
        "nic_transmit":  50,
    }
    
    def __init__(self):
        self._tick_stages: List[PipelineStage] = []
        self._all_reports: List[LatencyReport] = []
    
    def start_tick(self) -> None:
        """Begin timing a new tick-to-trade cycle."""
        self._tick_stages = []
    
    def record_stage(self, stage_name: str, actual_ns: int) -> None:
        """Record actual latency for a pipeline stage.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Create PipelineStage with budget from STAGE_BUDGETS,
        actual_ns as provided, and within_budget comparison.
        Append to _tick_stages.
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def finish_tick(self) -> LatencyReport:
        """Complete a tick and generate latency report.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Sum up all stage budgets and actuals.
        Create LatencyReport with within_budget = total_actual <= total_budget.
        Append to _all_reports.
        
        Returns:
            LatencyReport for this tick
        """
        pass  # YOUR CODE HERE
    
    def summary(self) -> dict:
        """Summary statistics across all ticks."""
        if not self._all_reports:
            return {"ticks": 0}
        totals = [r.total_actual_ns for r in self._all_reports]
        return {
            "ticks": len(totals),
            "avg_ns": sum(totals) // len(totals),
            "min_ns": min(totals),
            "max_ns": max(totals),
            "within_budget_pct": sum(1 for r in self._all_reports if r.within_budget) * 100 // len(self._all_reports),
        }


class PipelineSimulator:
    """End-to-end FPGA trading pipeline.
    
    Chains all stages: NIC -> Decode -> Book -> Strategy -> Risk -> Encode -> NIC
    
    This is the complete tick-to-trade path that makes or breaks
    a high-frequency trading system.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, reference_price: FixedPoint,
                 strategy_config: StrategyConfig,
                 risk_limits: RiskLimits):
        self.decoder = FeedDecoder()
        self.book = OrderBook(reference_price)
        self.strategy = TradingStrategy(strategy_config)
        self.risk_checker = RiskChecker(risk_limits)
        self.encoder = OrderEncoder()
        self.latency = LatencyAnalyzer()
        self._next_order_id = 1000000
        self.ticks_processed: int = 0
        self.orders_sent: int = 0
    
    def process_tick(self, raw_message: bytes, arrival_ns: int) -> Optional[LatencyReport]:
        """Process one complete tick-to-trade cycle.
        
        TODO: Implement this method.
        
        Steps:
        1. Start latency tick
        2. NIC Receive (50ns simulated)
        3. Feed Decode: decode raw_message
        4. Book Update: process decoded event
        5. Strategy: get book snapshot, generate quotes
        6. Risk Check: check each quote
        7. Order Encode: encode approved orders
        8. NIC Transmit (50ns simulated)
        9. Finish latency tick, return report
        
        If any stage fails (decode error, no quotes generated),
        still record the latency for completed stages.
        
        Returns:
            LatencyReport for this tick

milestones.testCode:
# --- Milestone 4 Tests: Risk Checker and Latency Analysis ---
import struct

limits = RiskLimits(
    max_position=5000,
    max_orders_per_second=1000,
    max_price_deviation_bps=100,
    max_notional=10000000000,
    min_quantity=1,
    max_quantity=10000
)
risk = RiskChecker(limits)
mid = FixedPoint(1500000)  # $150.00

# Test 1: Normal order passes all checks
normal_order = QuoteOrder(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1499900), 100)
result = risk.check_order(normal_order, 0, mid, 1000000000)
assert result.approved, f"Normal order should pass: {result.details}"
assert risk.orders_approved == 1, f"Expected 1 approved, got {risk.orders_approved}"
print("Test 1 passed: Normal order approved")

# Test 2: Position limit blocks oversized order
big_order = QuoteOrder(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1500000), 3000)
result2 = risk.check_order(big_order, 4000, mid, 2000000000)
assert not result2.approved, "Should reject: position would be 7000 > 5000"
assert result2.reject_reason == RiskRejectReason.POSITION_LIMIT
print("Test 2 passed: Position limit blocks oversized order")

# Test 3: Rate limiting
rate_risk = RiskChecker(RiskLimits(max_orders_per_second=5))
base_ts = 10_000_000_000
for i in range(5):
    small = QuoteOrder(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1500000), 10)
    r = rate_risk.check_order(small, 0, mid, base_ts + i)
assert rate_risk.orders_approved == 5
rate_result = rate_risk.check_order(small, 0, mid, base_ts + 6)
assert not rate_result.approved, "Should reject: rate limit exceeded"
assert rate_result.reject_reason == RiskRejectReason.RATE_LIMIT
print("Test 3 passed: Rate limiting works")

# Test 4: Price deviation check
far_price_order = QuoteOrder(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1400000), 100)  # $140, 6.67% away
result4 = risk.check_order(far_price_order, 0, mid, 5000000000)
assert not result4.approved, "Should reject: price too far from mid"
assert result4.reject_reason == RiskRejectReason.PRICE_DEVIATION
print("Test 4 passed: Price deviation check blocks far-away orders")

# Test 5: Notional limit
tiny_limits = RiskLimits(max_notional=100000000)  # $10K
huge_order = QuoteOrder(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1500000), 1000)  # $150K notional
notional_risk = RiskChecker(tiny_limits)
result5 = notional_risk.check_order(huge_order, 0, mid, 6000000000)
assert not result5.approved, "Should reject: notional too large"
assert result5.reject_reason == RiskRejectReason.NOTIONAL_LIMIT
print("Test 5 passed: Notional limit blocks large orders")

# Test 6: Order encoding
encoder = OrderEncoder()
order_to_encode = QuoteOrder(Side.BID, FixedPoint(1499850), 200)
encoded = encoder.encode_order(order_to_encode, 999001, 7000000000)
assert len(encoded) == OUTBOUND_SIZE, f"Expected {OUTBOUND_SIZE} bytes, got {len(encoded)}"
msg_type, oid, side, price, qty, ts = struct.unpack(OUTBOUND_FORMAT, encoded)
assert msg_type == MessageType.ADD, f"Expected ADD type, got {msg_type}"
assert oid == 999001, f"Expected order_id 999001, got {oid}"
assert price == 1499850, f"Expected price 1499850, got {price}"
assert qty == 200, f"Expected quantity 200, got {qty}"
assert encoder.messages_encoded == 1
print("Test 6 passed: Order encoding correct")

# Test 7: Latency budget analysis
analyzer = LatencyAnalyzer()
analyzer.start_tick()
analyzer.record_stage("nic_receive", 50)
analyzer.record_stage("feed_decode", 28)
analyzer.record_stage("book_update", 18)
analyzer.record_stage("strategy", 35)
analyzer.record_stage("risk_check", 14)
analyzer.record_stage("order_encode", 9)
analyzer.record_stage("nic_transmit", 48)
report = analyzer.finish_tick()
assert report.total_budget_ns == 215, f"Expected total budget 215ns, got {report.total_budget_ns}"
assert report.total_actual_ns == 202, f"Expected total actual 202ns, got {report.total_actual_ns}"
assert report.within_budget, "Should be within budget"
print("Test 7 passed: Latency budget analysis correct")

# Test 8: Pipeline end-to-end
pipeline = PipelineSimulator(
    reference_price=FixedPoint(1500000),
    strategy_config=StrategyConfig(),
    risk_limits=RiskLimits()
)
# Seed the book with some orders so strategy can generate quotes
for i in range(5):
    bid_msg = encode_message(MessageType.ADD, 8000 + i, Side.BID,
                             1499900 - i * 100, 100, i * 1000000)
    pipeline.process_tick(bid_msg, i * 1000000)
for i in range(5):
    ask_msg = encode_message(MessageType.ADD, 9000 + i, Side.ASK,
                             1500100 + i * 100, 100, (5 + i) * 1000000)
    report = pipeline.process_tick(ask_msg, (5 + i) * 1000000)
assert pipeline.ticks_processed > 0, "Pipeline should have processed ticks"
assert report is not None, "Should get latency report"
assert report.total_actual_ns == 215, f"Expected 215ns total, got {report.total_actual_ns}"
print("Test 8 passed: End-to-end pipeline works")

# Test 9: Latency summary
summary = pipeline.latency.summary()
assert summary["ticks"] > 0, "Should have tick records"
assert "avg_ns" in summary, "Summary should include avg_ns"
print("Test 9 passed: Latency summary available")

print("\n=== ALL MILESTONE 4 TESTS PASSED ===")
hints: For check_order: run all 5 checks sequentially (position, rate, price deviation, notional, quantity). Return the FIRST failure. In hardware these run in parallel, but our simulation can be sequential.
For rate limiting: keep a list of recent order timestamps. Before checking, filter out timestamps older than 1 second (1_000_000_000 ns) from the current timestamp.

For the pipeline: call each stage's method (decoder.decode_message, book.process_event, strategy.on_book_update, etc.) and record_stage with the simulated latency constant. If a stage produces no output (e.g., no quotes), still finish the tick with recorded stages.

marketDataFeed.description: 1000 market data events forming a realistic trading session for a stock around $150.00. The sequence includes opening book-building, steady-state trading with fills, a volatility spike, and end-of-day narrowing. 100 representative events are provided explicitly; the remaining 900 follow the described generation pattern.

generationPattern.priceModel: Random walk: price(t+1) = price(t) + N(0, tick_size) where tick_size = $0.01. Bounded to [$147.00, $153.00].
generationPattern.timestampModel: Inter-arrival: exponential(mean=100_000ns = 100us). Timestamps increment monotonically from 34_200_000_000_000 (09:30:00.000 EST).
generationPattern.orderIdModel: Sequential starting from 100001. MODIFY/CANCEL/EXECUTE reference previously ADDed order IDs that are still active.
generationPattern.quantityModel: Uniform(10, 500) for ADD. MODIFY quantities: Uniform(10, original_qty). EXECUTE quantities: Uniform(1, remaining_qty).
generationPattern.spreadModel: Base spread: 1-3 ticks ($0.01-$0.03). Widens to 5-10 ticks during volatility spike (events 600-700).

phases.description: Opening: book building. Mostly ADDs, spread narrowing from 5 ticks to 2 ticks.
Morning trading: balanced mix. Price drifts up from $150.00 to $150.50.
Midday: lower activity. Price stable around $150.30-$150.50.
Volatility spike: rapid price movement $150.50 -> $149.50 -> $150.20. High cancel/execute rate.

sampleEvents.note: Aggressive bid at $150.00 -- BBO crossing imminent
Aggressive ask at $150.00 -- spread to 1 tick
Fill remaining of original best bid
End of opening: bid at $150.00, transitioning to morning session

orderBookCheckpoints.description: Expected aggregate order book state at key event boundaries. These checkpoints validate correct event processing through the full sequence.

expectedBidDepthApprox.note: Accumulated bid liquidity across active levels

expectedAskDepthApprox.note: Accumulated ask liquidity across active levels

expectedBidLevels.note: Active bid price levels with quantity > 0

expectedAskLevels.note: Active ask price levels with quantity > 0

afterEvent100.narrative: Opening phase complete. Book has tightened from 10-tick spread to 1-2 ticks. Both sides have reasonable depth. Price centered at $149.99-$150.01.

afterEvent500.narrative: Morning session. Price has drifted up ~$0.20-$0.40 from open. Spread is tight. Good depth on both sides. Active trading with regular fills.

afterEvent1000.narrative: Session complete. Price recovered from mid-day volatility spike and stabilized. Spread is tight for closing. Total of ~450 ADDs, ~150 MODIFYs, ~200 CANCELs, ~200 EXECUTEs processed.

criteria.item: Feed decoder correctly parses all 4 message types from binary
Fixed-point arithmetic uses NO floating-point operations in any computation path
Order book correctly processes Add/Modify/Cancel/Execute events
Best bid/ask and spread are correct after every event
VWAP calculation uses integer arithmetic and produces correct result
Market-making quotes are symmetric around mid-price with zero inventory
Inventory skew adjusts correct side and clamps to minimum spread
All risk checks (position, rate, price, notional, quantity) function correctly
Per-stage latency tracking records all 7 pipeline stages
Total tick-to-trade budget calculation is correct (215ns)
Within-budget determination is accurate for each stage and overall
Latency summary statistics (avg, min, max, % within budget) are computed
Pipeline simulator chains all stages correctly end-to-end
Student can explain why each stage has its specific nanosecond budget
Pre-allocated array-based book structure (not tree/dict by price)
Clean separation between pipeline stages (decoder, book, strategy, risk)
Order map (CAM equivalent) for O(1) order lookup by ID
Strategy correctly decouples signal generation from execution
Risk checker runs all checks independently (parallel-ready design)
Code is well-structured with clear dataclass boundaries
Explains hardware constraints driving each design decision
Documents why fixed-point (not float) is required in trading
Describes the FPGA advantage over CPU/GPU for this pipeline
Comments explain the mapping from Python simulation to hardware blocks
